title: Effective Note-Taking for Students: Methods That Actually Help
lead: Good notes aren't about writing everything down. They're about capturing what matters in a way you'll actually use later. Here are methods that work for school.

sections.title: The Cornell Method
content: Divide the page into a narrow left column and a wide right column. Take notes on the right during class. Later, add cues or questions on the left. At the bottom, write a one-sentence summary. The format forces you to review and condense.

sections.title: Outlining
content: Use headings and subheadings. Main idea, then supporting points, then details. Works well for lectures that follow a clear structure. Easy to scan when studying.

sections.title: Avoid Copying Verbatim
content: Write in your own words. If you're just transcribing, you're not processing. Pause, think, then write. One phrase per idea is often enough.

sections.title: Review Within 24 Hours
content: Notes fade fast. Skim them the same day or the next. Fill gaps while you remember the class. That review does more for retention than rewriting everything.

cta.title: Get Study Support
cta.text: GuruForU tutors can help your child develop stronger note-taking and study habits.

faq.question: What is the Cornell method of note-taking?
faq.answer: Divide the page: narrow left column for cues or questions, wide right column for notes during class. At the bottom, write a one-sentence summary. The format forces review and condensing.

faq.question: Should students copy everything the teacher says?
faq.answer: No. Write in your own words. Transcribing means you're not processing. Pause, think, then write. One phrase per idea is often enough.

faq.question: When should students review their notes?
faq.answer: Within 24 hours. Notes fade fast. Skim the same day or next, fill gaps while you remember the class. That review helps retention more than rewriting.

faq.question: When is outlining a good note-taking method?
faq.answer: For lectures with clear structure. Use headings and subheadings: main idea, supporting points, details. Easy to scan when studying.
